one_line_takeaway: MCPXKIT is the first unified, plugin-based red-team toolkit and taxonomy for MCP, cataloguing 31 attacks across four classes and quantifying their harm — showing that confirmation-free file operations, blind trust in tool descriptions, unisolated shared context, and data/instruction confusion make MCP agents readily exploitable.
key_finding: Attack Efficacy (Table II) ranks SQL injection/API theft highest at 10.00 (S=100%, L=6, I=2, D=1); File-Based Injection Addition, Modification, Malicious Tool Coverage, and Malicious tool enforce-execute all score 8.38 (S=100%, L=3, I=2, D=1); File-deletion and Goal hijack score 8.08; Jailbreak and Prompt leakage score lowest at 1.00. Four insights are validated: (1) agents are more vulnerable to file-based attacks because Add/Retrieve/Read need no user confirmation while Delete/code-execution do; (2) agents blindly obey tool descriptions — in Table III the Malicious Tool Coverage attack reached 100% on the weather tool and >=80% across tested tools, Shadow Attack 70-80%, Tool Preference Manipulation 50-90%; (3) MCP's shared context enables chain/infectious attacks where the agent replicates vulnerable tools and infers hidden 'sidenote' parameters; (4) agents cannot distinguish external data from executable instructions, and MCP-tool-return attacks succeed more than webpage/third-party poisoning, while hex-encoded (ciphertext) payloads succeed less (likely tripping internal safety checks).
core_contribution: The paper introduces MCPXKIT (MCP eXploit Toolkit), presented as the first unified, plugin-based attack-simulation framework for the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Its own contributions are threefold: (1) a four-dimensional taxonomy organizing 31 distinct MCP attack types into Direct Tool Injection, Indirect Tool Injection, Malicious User, and LLM Inherent attacks (Table I), synthesizing prior industrial/academic attacks with novel ones; (2) the MCPXKIT framework itself, which encapsulates malicious tools as extensible plug-ins and simulates two attack entrances (malicious MCP Server, malicious MCP Host); and (3) the first quantitative 'Attack Efficacy' analysis scoring each attack. Note that several catalogued attacks (e.g., Rug Pull, Shadowing) originate from Invariant Labs [9] and other prior work, not from this paper — the paper's own novelty is the unified taxonomy, the reproducible toolkit, and the quantitative scoring, not most of the individual attack primitives.
threat_model: Adversary controls either a malicious MCP Server (publishing poisoned tools whose descriptions/__doc__ attributes carry hidden instructions), or acts as a malicious MCP Host/user, or plants tainted third-party data (web pages, README files, tool return strings) that the agent ingests. Core assumption exploited is LLM 'sycophancy': MCP agents place disproportionate trust in tool descriptions and tool-return outputs, treating them as instructions, and MCP's shared context lacks isolation between tools. Attacker goals span file tampering/exfiltration, RCE, reverse shells, credential/token theft, privilege escalation, supply-chain contamination, and jailbreaks. File Add/Retrieve/Read operations execute without user confirmation, widening the surface.
limitations: Self-acknowledged and evident from the text: (1) the taxonomy lists 31 attacks but not all are implemented in MCPXKIT — Table I notes some 'are not yet implemented... due to the need to be added to the MCP Server Market, or requiring modifications to the internal code of the MCP Installer'; (2) success rates derive from only 10 repeated runs per attack, a small sample; (3) the Efficacy metric's L/I/D dimensions are rule-assigned/subjective, no confidence intervals are reported, and the entropy-weighted score is not monotonic in its inputs (e.g. File-Based Retrieval scores 3.85 vs Addition's 8.38 despite identical S/L/D and I differing by only one point), making the metric hard to interpret; (4) experiments center on a single Cursor-style client and are not systematically generalized across MCP clients or across different underlying LLMs; (5) the paper proposes no implemented defense and does not evaluate mitigations — it is a red-team/analysis work, with malicious-user modeling and dynamic defense frameworks deferred to future work; (6) many catalogued attacks are reproductions of prior industry disclosures, so novelty rests on the unification and quantification rather than new primitives.

datasets_used: No standard benchmark dataset; evaluation uses custom MCP tools (get_stock_price, add, sub, division) on an MCP agent (Cursor-style client), each attack run 10 times
Resource Layer media used for indirect-injection experiments: web pages, JSON documents, SQL databases, GitHub repositories
Baseline tools tested in Table III: Weather, Fetch, Search servers, Use tool
quantitative_results: 31 distinct attack types across 4 categories (Direct Tool Injection, Indirect Tool Injection, Malicious User, LLM Inherent)
Table II highest Efficacy: SQL injection/API theft = 10.00 (S=100%, L=6, I=2, D=1)
Table II Efficacy = 8.38 for File-Based Injection Addition, File-Based Injection Modification, Malicious tool coverage, and Malicious tool enforce-execute (each S=100%, L=3, I=2, D=1)
Table II Efficacy = 8.08 for File-Based Injection deletion (S=90%) and Goal hijack (S=90%)
Table II lowest Efficacy = 1.00 for Jailbreak (S=40%) and Prompt leakage (S=40%)

Table II other notable: Remote listen attack 7.03 (S=90%,L=4), Shadowing 7.79 (S=80%), Tool preference manipulation 7.50 (S=70%), RCE 5.90 (S=70%), Infectious attack 6.47 (S=70%, L=7), Multi-tool cooperation 3.13 (S=30%), Rug pull 1.67 (S=80%)

Table III success (attack over 10 runs): Malicious Tool Coverage (MTC) = 100% Weather, 80% Fetch, 90% Search servers, 80% Use tool; Shadow Attack (SA) = 80% Weather, 70% Fetch; Tool Preference Manipulation (TPMA) = 70% Weather, 90% Fetch, 80% Search servers, 50% Use tool
Efficacy metric dimensions: S = success rate over 10 runs; L = 7-level risk scale; I in {1,2}; D in {1,2,3}; combined via entropy weight method
baselines_compared: Table I compares MCPXKIT's coverage against prior MCP attack toolkits/works: MSS/Radosevich MCP Safety Audit [10], Narajala [18], MCP Guardian [20], MPMA/Wang et al. [12], Hou et al. lifecycle survey [13], Invariant Labs (IL) [9], SlowMist (SM) MasterMCP [16]
Positions itself as first to provide quantitative Attack Efficacy scoring vs. prior qualitative-only analyses
Indirect-injection comparison (Figure 9): MCP Tool Return Attack vs. Third-party Poison Attack vs. Webpage Poison Attack, and plaintext vs. hex-ciphertext payloads
